Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to put in around 30 hours a week, but it was scattered all over the place depending on what else was going on. I'd usually read through materials late at night when things were quieter, since that's when I could actually focus. With 13 online courses to juggle, I never really had a set schedule—it was more about survival than organization.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot based on deadlines and how overwhelmed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd barely touch certain courses, then cram everything in when assignments were due. I tried to be consistent, but with limited resources and so many courses, it felt impossible to maintain any real regularity. The stress made it hard to stick to any plan I made.
Q3: Usually what got me to log in was either a deadline approaching or seeing a notification about something new. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, since my attendance was pretty low and I worried about falling behind. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more because there was something kind of satisfying about completing those challenges.
Q4: For a while, the gamified elements made logging in feel more routine—like I wanted to see my progress and earn those points or badges. It became a bit of a habit to check in and see where I stood compared to before. But that wore off when the stress piled up and I started feeling bored with the whole thing. Eventually it just became another task rather than something I looked forward to.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me since I learn best through reading and writing things out. I also found the discussion posts helpful because seeing other students' perspectives helped me understand concepts I was struggling with. The videos were okay, but I preferred having text I could go back to and highlight or take notes from.
Q6: I often skipped or delayed the video lectures because they took too long and I couldn't easily search through them for specific information. Some of the optional readings I ignored completely because I was already drowning in required content. With 13 courses, I had to make choices about what to prioritize, and anything that seemed redundant got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a clear connection to assignments or exams, or if it helped me understand something I was genuinely confused about. The gamified tasks were interesting at first because they broke up the monotony and gave me small wins. But if something felt like busywork with no real payoff, I'd lose interest fast—I just didn't have the energy to waste.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford to spend time on. Required ones had deadlines and consequences, so they automatically got priority even when I was exhausted. Sometimes I'd look at optional stuff and think 'that looks interesting' but then reality would hit and I'd focus on what was mandatory. The pressure of required work made optional content feel almost irrelevant.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download PDFs and readings to my laptop so I could study offline without using data or needing internet. I also wrote out notes by hand and used those to review because that helps me remember things better. Sometimes I'd find explanations on YouTube or other sites that made more sense than the course materials.
Q10: There were many times I was studying from printed notes or downloaded files for hours, but the LMS wouldn't show any of that. I'd also work through problems on paper or reread my handwritten summaries before exams. My peer interactions often happened through messaging apps rather than the LMS discussion boards. So my actual study time was way higher than what any log would capture.
Q11: Definitely. I'd access a reading or resource once, download it, and then work with it offline for days. With my limited internet access and resources, it made more sense to grab everything I needed in one session and then study without being connected. The LMS probably showed one quick login, but I'd spend hours with that same material afterwards.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. The anxiety was overwhelming and I felt completely burnt out from trying to keep up with so many courses. I'd sit down to study and just feel this crushing boredom mixed with panic—like nothing I did mattered anyway. My attendance dropped even more during those periods because I couldn't face logging in and seeing how behind I was.
Q13: Absolutely. The workload from 13 courses was insane, and I was constantly confused about priorities. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities and limited resources meant I often had to choose between courses, and some inevitably got neglected. The fatigue made everything feel ten times harder.
Q14: There were plenty of times I'd be scrolling through materials or watching videos but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd realize I'd read the same paragraph five times without absorbing anything because I was too anxious or bored. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open for an hour but couldn't tell you what I'd actually learned. It was like going through the motions without being present.
Q15: I postponed assignments all the time, even important ones. Part of it was the anxiety—starting something meant facing how much I didn't understand, which was terrifying. Sometimes I was just too bored or burnt out to care, even though I knew I should. The combination of high stress and low motivation made procrastination feel like the only way to cope, even though it made things worse.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd ignore the LMS for days, then frantically log in and try to complete everything at the last minute. The gamified tasks helped a bit because they created smaller, more frequent goals, but the big assignment deadlines still drove most of my activity. Without a deadline looming, I struggled to find any motivation to engage with the material.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When I got constructive comments, it sometimes motivated me to try harder on the next assignment. But when feedback was delayed or just a grade with no explanation, it felt pointless and demotivating. Honestly, by the end of the semester, I'd kind of stopped expecting feedback to help me improve—I was just trying to survive.
Q18: The course structure with so many online components made it easy to fall behind without realizing it. Having everything accessible online was supposed to be convenient, but it also meant there was no external structure forcing me to stay on track. The gamified elements added some structure, but it wasn't enough to overcome my attendance issues and the sheer volume of content across all my courses.
Q19: The gamified tasks actually made engagement easier at first—they broke things into smaller pieces and gave me a sense of accomplishment. But the overall amount of content and the lack of clear guidance made things harder. When I was confused about what to prioritize or how concepts connected, I'd just feel paralyzed. Better signposting of what was truly essential would have helped a lot.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well actually. I had decent peer interactions and watched videos and participated in discussions, but my assignment completion was low because I couldn't translate that activity into finished work. Sometimes you're trying to understand things but the actual output—the assignments—feels impossible when you're anxious and overwhelmed. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That's kind of the opposite of my situation, but I can see how it happens. Some people are efficient—they know exactly what they need, grab it quickly, and study effectively offline. They don't need to spend hours on the LMS to learn the material. My problem was I spent time on the LMS but couldn't convert that into good exam performance because of how scattered and stressed I was.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and how long you stayed. But they miss everything that happens offline—the handwritten notes, the downloaded materials studied for hours, the conversations with classmates outside the platform. They also can't show whether you were actually engaged mentally or just going through the motions while your mind was elsewhere.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling like it matters. It's when you're curious and want to understand, not just complete tasks to check boxes. For me, real engagement happened rarely this semester—mostly I was just trying to get through things. True engagement requires mental energy and motivation that I didn't have enough of.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I was dealing with, or how bored and burnt out I felt despite putting in effort. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials or handwritten notes. It also can't show that I actually wanted to do well but was overwhelmed by 13 courses with limited resources. The human struggle behind the numbers is completely invisible.
